Auckland D365 Business Central User Group

Building Integrations That
Survive the Real World

AI, trust, and lessons learned. The Business Central integration patterns that hold up in production, and an honest look at what happens when you let AI build one end to end.
Tharanga Chandrasekara
Head of Technology & Product, Equerra · Microsoft MVP
Integrations look great in the demo and fall apart in production. First half: the patterns. Second half: I hand the whole thing to AI and see if I would ship what it wrote.
Who is talking

Tharanga Chandrasekara

  • Head of Technology and Product at Equerra
  • Microsoft MVP for the past 12 years
  • Co-organizer of the Auckland D365 BC User Group and DOK ANZ
Keep it short. Co-organiser of this group, so it is home turf.
Most integrations look great in the demo.
Then they meet production at 2am.

And now AI writes the integration for you. It compiles. It demos. Would you ship it?

01
Production is the real test, and AI raises the stakes because it produces confident code fast.

Patterns? That was 2024.
Now you just need AI.
A tongue-in-cheek provocation to open the section. Everyone is being told AI makes the old discipline optional. Let it land, then spend the next few minutes quietly proving why the patterns still matter.
The shape of the problem

A typical BC integration layer

A common shape: an external storefront, a 3PL warehouse (WMS), Business Central as the hub. This whole layer is what the AI is building right now.
Design to survive failure

Five ways integrations break

Problem 1

Everything is coupled

Direct calls tie BC to every system. No log, and a retry means the source resends.

Problem 2

The same message twice

Sources retry, crashed polls re-pull. The same order becomes a second sales order.

Problem 3

Failures vanish

Errors sit unread. You cannot tell which failure is common, or that it happened.

Problem 4

Answers take too long

Some work runs for minutes or needs a human. The inbound call times out waiting.

Problem 5

One failure stalls the batch

One pull fans out to hundreds. Row 47 fails and takes the whole run down.

One pattern each

Five problems, five patterns, all on one staging table. One more column each.

The map for the section. Five failure modes, one pattern for each, all built on a single Integration Message table. Now walk each: the problem, then the fix.
The problem

Direct API calls do not survive

  • Tight coupling. Change one system and you break many
  • No single log. Nothing says what came in and what happened
  • Retry means resend. The source has to send it all again, and often will not
The pain of wiring BC straight to another system. The fix is a place to put the message before you act on it.
The pattern · 1 of 5

Integration Message: stage first, act later

  • The call only stores a record, then returns
  • A Job Queue dispatches by Type and does the work
  • Extend with one new enum value plus one codeunit
The Integration Message table
FieldType
Message ID (PK)Guid
Document No. (SK)Text[50]
TypeEnum
StatusEnum
Request ContentBlob
Response ContentBlob
1 Integration Message2 Duplicate Detection3 Error Classification4 Delayed Reply5 Durable Workflow
The foundation and the base schema. The call returns the instant the row is written; everything risky happens later. Each of the next four patterns adds exactly one thing to this same table.
The problem

The same order just arrived. Twice.

  • Sources retry until they are acknowledged
  • A crashed poll re-pulls the same orders next run
  • Without protection, that is a second sales order and an angry customer
None of these are bugs on the source side. They are correct behaviour. So the duplicate has to be handled on our side.
The pattern · 2 of 5

Duplicate Detection: an Idempotency Key

  • Take a stable id from the source as the key
  • Check it before processing; if done, return the same result
  • The caller cannot tell the first attempt from the tenth
Adds: Idempotency Key
FieldType
Message ID (PK)Guid
Document No. (SK)Text[50]
TypeEnum
StatusEnum
Request ContentBlob
Response ContentBlob
Idempotency Key (SK)Text[50]
1 Integration Message2 Duplicate Detection3 Error Classification4 Delayed Reply5 Durable Workflow
Same key, same result. The second attempt is a no-op, no second sales order. One new row on the table.
The problem

The integration failed. Nobody noticed.

  • Errors sit unread in a mailbox nobody watches
  • No way to tell which failure is most common
  • The customer finds it before you do
The goal is triage. Turn a wall of stack traces into a small set of named, countable codes.
The pattern · 3 of 5

Error Classification: name every failure

  • Record the error on the message, mark it Failed
  • Classify in the background, off the hot path (AI is fine here)
  • Named, countable Error Codes you can triage
Adds: Error Content, Error Code
FieldType
Message ID (PK)Guid
Document No. (SK)Text[50]
TypeEnum
StatusEnum
Request ContentBlob
Response ContentBlob
Idempotency Key (SK)Text[50]
Error ContentBlob
Error CodeCode[20]
1 Integration Message2 Duplicate Detection3 Error Classification4 Delayed Reply5 Durable Workflow
Classification is out of band, so a slow classifier never blocks processing. Two new rows on the table.
The problem

The answer takes minutes. The caller waited seconds.

  • Some processes run for minutes, not milliseconds
  • The inbound call times out waiting for the answer
  • Human review cannot fit inside one request
Accept fast, reply later. The trick that ties the later reply back is a Correlation ID.
The pattern · 4 of 5

Delayed Reply: a Correlation ID

  • Accept fast, reply later when it is ready
  • A Correlation ID ties the reply to the request
  • One trace across systems and across time
Adds: Correlation ID
FieldType
Message ID (PK)Guid
Document No. (SK)Text[50]
TypeEnum
StatusEnum
Request ContentBlob
Response ContentBlob
Idempotency Key (SK)Text[50]
Error ContentBlob
Error CodeCode[20]
Correlation ID (SK)Guid
1 Integration Message2 Duplicate Detection3 Error Classification4 Delayed Reply5 Durable Workflow
Accept fast, reply later; the Correlation ID ties them together. This is the traceability support pulls up at 2am: one id, one story, end to end. One new row.
The problem

One pull became 300 orders. Number 47 failed.

  • One pull fans out into hundreds of orders
  • A mid-batch failure stalls everything behind it
  • No trace of what triggered what
The naive version is one giant loop in one transaction. Row 47 fails and you lose the lot.
The pattern · 5 of 5

Durable Workflow: small atomic steps

  • Each step is its own message: atomic, retryable
  • One failure is retried alone, it does not block the rest
  • Parent Message ID links each step back to the pull
Adds: Parent Message ID
FieldType
Message ID (PK)Guid
Document No. (SK)Text[50]
TypeEnum
StatusEnum
Request ContentBlob
Response ContentBlob
Idempotency Key (SK)Text[50]
Error ContentBlob
Error CodeCode[20]
Correlation ID (SK)Guid
Parent Message IDGuid
1 Integration Message2 Duplicate Detection3 Error Classification4 Delayed Reply5 Durable Workflow
Each order runs on its own. Order 47 fails and is retried alone while the rest finish. The Parent Message ID is the final row: it links every step back to the pull, so you can trace the whole tree.
The pattern · 5 of 5

One fails, the rest go through

Rows are orders, columns are stages. Order 2 fails at Create order and never reaches Release, yet Orders 1 and 3 finish.
1 Integration Message2 Duplicate Detection3 Error Classification4 Delayed Reply5 Durable Workflow
The grid says it in one glance: each order is a row, each stage a column. Order 2 dies at Create order and never reaches Release, but Orders 1 and 3 sail through. That is fault isolation. One giant transaction would have taken all three down.
Inbound · Webhooks

Business Central subscribes to an external webhook

The storefront fires events. BC subscribes and receives them directly. Will this work?
Step 1 of 3. Frame it as a question to the room: the storefront offers webhooks, so can BC just subscribe and receive them? Take a few answers, then advance to show what actually happens.
Inbound · Webhooks

BC is not a webhook receiver

But BC has no listener for an unauthenticated generic POST. It never arrives.
Step 2 of 3. It cannot. There is no AL listener for a raw external POST, so the message dies at the door.
Inbound · Webhooks

Alternative ways to subscribe to external webhooks

Azure Functions

An HTTP-triggered function is the receiver. Authenticate, transform, and call the BC staging API.

Logic Apps or Power Automate

A low-code webhook trigger subscribes, then posts to BC. Fast to stand up, easy to monitor.

API Management

A managed front door. Validates the signature, applies policy and throttling, forwards to BC.

Custom Integration Service

Your own middleware when you need full control of auth, mapping, and retry.

Whichever you pick, the job is the same: subscribe, authenticate, then push to the BC staging API.
Step 3. The receiver is never BC itself. Walk the options: Functions and Logic Apps for most cases, APIM when you already run it, a custom service when you need control. All of them do the same three things.
Inbound · Polling

Polling: never lose your place

  • A framing record stores the cursor: the last successful fetch time
  • Each poll fetches only since the cursor, then commits the new one
  • A crashed poll never commits, so the next poll refetches from the last good cursor, nothing is lost
  • Windows overlap the boundary on purpose; duplicates are caught by the Idempotency Key
Walk the timeline. 1:00 poll succeeds and commits the cursor at 1:00. The 2:00 poll crashes before it commits, so the cursor stays at 1:00. The 3:00 poll reads that cursor and fetches everything from 1:00 to 3:00, so the crashed window is covered and nothing is missed. Overlap at the boundary is fine because the Idempotency Key absorbs the duplicates.
Outbound · Business Events

Notify, do not deliver

  • Business-process driven, not record driven. A notification, not the payload
  • One event can fan out to many subscribers, no handshake to manage
  • Honest lesson: on Black Friday, delivery lagged by minutes
  • Fine for "eventually". If it must be instant, use a request
The cleanest outbound shape: fire and forget, anyone subscribes. But it is eventual. On the busiest day of the year that eventual became minutes. Match the mechanism to the promise.
Outbound · Requests

Stage the work, never call from posting

  • Posting only stages a message, then returns
  • The Job Queue sends it, outside the posting transaction
  • Idempotency-Key and Correlation ID on every send
  • Response recorded back on the message
  • Never hold a connection open inside posting
An external call inside the posting transaction holds locks and fails the post when the third party is down. Stage it, then send from the Job Queue where it can retry safely. This is the anti-pattern AI loves to write.
The modern twist
Let's see how AI can help us.
Introduce the AI-built integration and set up the honest scorecard: where it helped, and where it was confidently wrong.
Honest look · the good

Where AI genuinely helped

Scaffolding, fast

The Integration Message table, the Type enum, the interface and its dispatch. Minutes, not hours.

Boilerplate it never forgets

API page, Job Queue skeletons, permission sets, captions and tooltips. The tedious, correct plumbing.

A plausible first draft

Given the patterns as context, it reproduced the shape of a decent integration surprisingly well.

Explaining itself

It documented what it wrote, which made reviewing far quicker than reviewing a stranger's code.

Give real credit. As a fast muscle for scaffolding, AI is excellent.
Honest look · the confidently wrong

Where it broke the rules, cheerfully

  • HttpClient inside OnAfterPostSalesDoc: an external call in the posting transaction, written with total confidence
  • No idempotency: a retry would happily create a second sales order
  • Swallowed errors: caught, logged to nowhere, message left looking Completed
  • A synchronous wait loop in an API handler, holding the request open
  • Direct posting from the poll handler: no staging, no retry boundary

Every one compiled. Every one demos fine. So how do you catch this, reliably, every time?

Each compiled, each demos on one happy record, each is a 2am page. The AI was never uncertain. That question sets up the check: something that reviews against a shared, vetted standard.
github.com/microsoft/BCQuality

The check: BCQuality

A curated, machine-readable knowledge base and skills library for Business Central that AI agents and tools consume to apply one consistent quality standard.

Introduce it only now. Not a linter, not a style guide. Shared, vetted BC knowledge in a form machines can act on. The admission test is why it is dense with real traps.
How it is structured

Three layers, one knowledge base

Microsoft for platform guardrails, Community for ecosystem patterns, Custom for your own rules.
The layering is why it scales. Your house rules live in Custom and the same agents pick them up.
The point of the whole talk

Using the patterns with BCQuality

The spec is the brain. AI is the muscle. BCQuality is the gate. Loop until the findings are clean, then it is trusted.
The centerpiece. Patterns define the standard. AI implements. BCQuality reviews and cites a rule. Fix and re-run until clean. Nothing merges on confidence.
Enough slides.
Let me show you it running.

The repo, the verifier agents, and the findings on the code AI just wrote.

Live: the repo
Switch to the repo. Run the verifier agents against the AI's code. Show the findings: each names the rule it breaks and cites the knowledge file. Show the HttpClient-in-posting block, then the fix, then a clean re-run.
The verdict

Would I trust it in production?

Yes, after the guardrails.
Never on its confidence alone.
  • AI is a fast muscle, not the brain
  • The patterns are the standard you hold it to
  • BCQuality turns "looks fine" into "provably fine"
  • Shipping its first draft is how you get the 2am page
Land the plane. AI plus the gate is a genuine accelerator. AI minus the gate is a confident liability.
Run it yourself

  • Built with Spec-Driven Development: spec first, then code
  • The verifier agents from this talk, wired to BCQuality
  • The same anti-patterns, caught by the same gates
